title: Multiline copying doesn't work properly
author: NSPC911
body:
For some reason, every time I select multiple lines to copy, it never copies the last three characters per line

But it works fine if I select a single line

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/76b0176e-ed2e-4bf5-9ff5-5a1ce27021d2

title: Allow X11 primary selection copy/paste
author: twopwood
body: Is it possible to disable the functionality where selecting text with a mouse copies it to the main clipboard and go back to the standard X11 way where selecting text doesn't use the main clipboard and middle clicking pastes the primary selection buffer?  I'm so used to the "standard" way that I keep clobbering my clipboard buffer when I just want to highlight text.  Maybe this could be an option in the `ClipboardMethod` config setting?  Thanks!

title: Error: failed to set key bindings for [word_wrap_mode] unknown action
author: pcwilcox
body:
Getting this error on a fresh install using the provided example configuration with `delta`, using either the example `ov.yaml` or `ov-less.yaml` config files:

```
% git diff
Error: failed to set key bindings for [word_wrap_mode] unknown action
```
Commenting out the relevant keybind from the config file eliminates the error. I'm not sure if this is related or not, but using the keybinds `w` or `W`  puts `Set WrapMode false` or `Set WrapMode true` but this doesn't seem to have any impact on what is shown on screen.

Running on a 2024 Macbook Pro, Taho 26.3.1, zsh, installed via HomeBrew:
```
% ov --version
ov version 0.51.1 rev:Homebrew
% brew info ov
==> ov ✔: stable 0.51.1 (bottled), HEAD
Feature-rich terminal-based text viewer
https://noborus.github.io/ov/
Installed
/opt/homebrew/Cellar/ov/0.51.1 (10 files, 7.9MB) *
  Poured from bottle using the formulae.brew.sh API on 2026-03-11 at 14:59:54
From: https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/blob/HEAD/Formula/o/ov.rb
License: MIT
==> Dependencies
Build: go ✔
==> Options
--HEAD
        Install HEAD version
==> Analytics
install: 177 (30 days), 319 (90 days), 1,282 (365 days)
install-on-request: 177 (30 days), 319 (90 days), 1,282 (365 days)
build-error: 0 (30 days)
```

title: "-X" does not work with "--follow-mode" or "--follow-name" when the content is smaller than the screen.
author: noborus
body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/noborus/ov/discussions/971
Originally posted by **HippoPerson** February 2, 2026 When exiting an ov session where "--follow-mode" or "--follow-name" has been specified, the screen clears, even when "-X" is also specified. Is there any way to get "-X" to work with "--follow-mode" and "--follow-screen"? Or is there any other way to prevent the screen from clearing when exiting a "--follow-mode" or "--follow-screen" session? And "-X" indeed does properly prevent screen clearing for me when exiting an ov session without "--follow-mode" nor "--follow-name".

title: when outputting a single page (with --quit if one screen), the output is limited to be 80 column wide
author: MinekPo1
body:
steps to reproduce:

- `printf 'a%.0s' $(seq $COLUMNS) | ov -F`

this should print a single line of `a`s, however depending on the width of the terminal, that line will be split into several. this is not solved by adding `-w=false` as that clips the line instead of wrapping it.

title: Emacs (& vi?) bindings in the command & filter ui
author: btakita
body:
`zsh`, `bash`, & `fish` setups often support emacs bindings. One thing that could be better with `less` and subsequently `ov` is the ability to kill region forward `M-d` and kill region backward `C-w`.

Given that `ov` is a terminal pager and works in context of a terminal shell, supporting the same subset of emacs bindings in the command & filter mode would be awesome. I mainly use kill region backward/forward, but some of the other bindings would also be nice.

I suppose some people prefer `vi` mode. I think the majority of people use emacs mode though. I like `vim` myself, but use emacs mode in the shell.

title: Feature Request: Bind `'` to open an interactive display of the marks in the document...Select mark by absolute position or navigation key bindings
author: btakita
body:
`less` supports assigning a key to a mark using `m<alphanumeric>` and jumping to a mark using `'<alphanumeric>`. While ov's mark is simpler and more streamlined (next/prev)...there are cases when it would be useful to jump to a particular mark.

It would be useful to jump to the absolutely positioned mark: `'<mark position>` or `'=<mark position>` (jump to the 2nd mark) using the positional ordering of the marks.

Another variation could be to jump n steps.

Perhaps `'+3` (jump next 3) and `'-5` (jump prev 5). This could be confusing with cycling though.

Navigating through would also be cool. Not sure what the correct shorcut would be but something like `'<Page Up>` to go jump to the previously selected mark and `'<Page Down>` to jump forward in the history.

title: Feature Request: Chain multiple filters
author: btakita
body:
It could be great to chain multiple filters in the command mode. One way could be:

`&filter1!exclusion1&filter2!exclusion2`

title: Suggestion: Option to wrap by words instead of characters
author: Maple38
body: In my opinion this looks a lot better. Less has it as an option, it would be great if Ov could too.

title: [FR] Allow shift-click to extend anchored selection
author: cyclic-elevator
body:
Hi, this pager combines:
- the ability to use mouse-select and line number exclusion to copy original text like in editors (nvim/helix)
- the ability to render ANSI color sequences

which is wonderful.

For better experience in mouse-select-copy, I would like to see the ability to apply the standard anchor-and-extend-selection-with-shift-click workflows supported by many editors.

title: Add terminal title setting option
author: noborus
body:
## Feature Request: Terminal Title Setting

### Problem
Currently, when using `ov` to view files, the terminal title remains unchanged. This makes it difficult to identify which file is being viewed when multiple terminal tabs/windows are open.

### Proposed Solution
Add a configuration option to automatically set the terminal title to display the current filename or caption.

### Implementation Details
- Add `TerminalTitle` boolean option to `General` config
- When enabled, set terminal title using escape sequences (`\033]0;title\007`)
- Use `displayTitle()` (filename or caption if set) as the title
- Default: `false` (disabled) to maintain current behavior

### Configuration Example
```yaml
General:
  TerminalTitle: true

title: Feature request: Allow adjusting the scroll multiplier
author: deathtrip
body:
Currently the scroll multiplier is locked at 2 lines.
As most terminals allow adjusting such value, it makes scrolling in ov inconsistent for those that set it higher in the terminal.
It would also complement the setting of the horizontal scroll width.

title: ov -F drops the last line if output is equal to the screen size
author: maraino
body:
### Description

`ov -F` drops the last line if the number of lines is equivalent to the terminal size.

A simple way to reproduce it is to create terminal with 3 lines.

If we do `printf "1\n2\n3\n"` the terminal will show something like this:

```console
2
3
$
```

If we scroll up we can see the 1.

But if we do `printf "1\n2\n3\n" | ov -F`, ov quits and 3 is dropped:

```console
1
2
$
```

title: Feature request: "Sticky" follow mode
author: maxeonyx
body:
When evaluating `ov` for a tool to watch live logs, I noticed that follow mode forces the view to the end as new lines arrive. If I try to scroll back to read or search recent output, `ov` immediately jumps to the bottom every time new data comes in, which makes it hard to inspect anything in an active stream.

I was looking for a tool (`less` doesn't work for this either - it doesn't allow navigation at all in follow mode) that works like a terminal emulator - to start with, it follows, then if you scroll away from the bottom you stay where you are, but when you scroll back down following resumes.

Example that reproduces the problem:

```sh
random_nonsense_loop() {
  trap 'echo -e "\nExiting cleanly."; exit 0' SIGINT
  while true; do
    tr -dc '[:graph:]' < /dev/urandom | head -c $((RANDOM % 60 + 20))
    echo
    sleep 0.2
  done
}

random_nonsense_loop | ov -f
```

So my request: add (or change the existing follow mode) a “sticky follow” mode that follows output when I’m at the bottom, pauses following when I scroll or search, and automatically resumes following if I return to the end. I would use that.

title: QuitSmall only works if EOF is received within 5-10ms
author: amonks
body:
With QuitSmall enabled, ov appears to only quit if the EOF is received within a few milliseconds.

Here's a small go program I wrote for testing this:

```go
// install this as `delay`
package main

import (
        "flag"
        "io"
        "os"
        "time"
)

func main() {
        // Accept delay in milliseconds (default 0)
        ms := flag.Int("ms", 0, "delay before forwarding EOF (in milliseconds)")
        flag.Parse()

        // Copy stdin to stdout directly
        _, err := io.Copy(os.Stdout, os.Stdin)
        if err != nil {
                os.Exit(1)
        }

        // When EOF is reached, wait
        time.Sleep(time.Duration(*ms) * time.Millisecond)
}
```

This invocation consistently quits:

```
cat short-file.txt | delay -ms=5 | ov --quit-if-one-screen
```

Whereas this one (at least on my machine) consistently does not quit:

```
cat short-file.txt | delay -ms=10 | ov --quit-if-one-screen
```

If I'm reading the code correctly, we only check docSmall() once during program initialization, and so the QuitSmall behavior depends on whether we happen to have received EOF by the time we hit that line. I suspect we should re-evaluate docSmall() when we _do_ receive EOF.

My use case here is `jj status` (where jj is an alternative to git, and I have `ov` configured as my pager because of its excellent mouse support). I'd like it to _always_ QuitSmall, since the output is never screen-sized, but in practice it appears to depend on the size of the repo I'm working in: in some repos, `jj status` exits quickly enough to trigger the quitSmall behavior, and in other repos it takes a bit longer and ov doesn't quit. The inconsistency is very frustrating.

title: [FR] Smart Headers, similar to Smart Columns (column-width mode)
author: ninetailedtori
body: It would be cool to have something that could reasonably guess what's the initial header, columnated material, what's a possible "section header", etc. Possible test cases here are things such as man pages, which I can show you how it renders currently without intelligence, and manual statement of variables. As described, I'd like the software to detect the top 6 lines should be "header", and thus declare it as such. I'd then like the lines numbered 1 and 6 in this, to read as section headers. We can see they differ in style, whether things such as a colon followed by a newline, or just that it doesn't abide by the columnation of the other lines. And then, the columns as follows: "-x, --long-x, desc" should be properly handled, because currently it's not intelligent enough to divide them. I've tested with both `ColumnWidth: true`, and `ColumnDelimiter: '/\s{4,}/'` This is with manual passing of arg -H6: This is without anything set, just my logical config, which has a boring -H3 because it works for the majority of logs and output scripts, but I'd like to be able to set it to -1 to auto-detect:

title: [FR] ClippedHighlight/CopiedHighlight option for formatting copied content.
author: ninetailedtori
body: It would be cool to be able to format text that was selected and copied, so you could make it underlined and blink to show you exactly what you copied. It would also be cool if the copy happened immediately rather than having to click off the selected text after the copy action, but I fear that's a different problem I'm having?

title: Ref #155, #156: automatically jump to the new section when watch reloads the buffer.
author: ninetailedtori
body: This should be the behaviour by default, unless scrolled at all, and a hotkey ideally to jump back to the current section. It might have been done already, but I'm not too sure about how to implement it in my config right now.

title: [FR] Support Wayland clipboard buffer
author: ninetailedtori
body: I do have the documentation for the clipboard buffer for Wayland, and it would be useful as most mainstream comps for Wayland support the [wlr-data-control-unstable-v1 protocol](https://wayland.app/protocols/wlr-data-control-unstable-v1) now. However I'm pretty dreadful at Golang, so would prefer if it was implemented by someone who knows what they're doing hahah. [Emersion's blog post on it](https://emersion.fr/blog/2020/wayland-clipboard-drag-and-drop/) is a solid guide to implementing the Wayland clipboard protocol, though in C/++ (my main language hahah).

title: Copying copies the plaintext buffer and not the pager output.
author: ninetailedtori
body:
This is with all clipboard methods, thus it likely is before, at the copy function itself. This can easily be seen with a header set and columnisation, due to it not actually copying the paged buffer but the original plaintext buffer passed into ov at the terminal location range selected. Thus the actually copied text and the intended selected pager content are not equal.

I'll post an image with an example as well.
